analysis_result: ANOVA p-value = 3.54e-08 (F-statistic = 9.39, df between=6, df within=101) comparing the methylation-derived Immune Score across the 7 methylation subgroups (G1-G7) in the Toronto 108-sample discovery cohort. Immune Score was computed as the LUMP-based (Aran 2015) leukocyte-hypomethylation proxy, since a dedicated ESTIMATE-methylation or MethylCIBERSORT deconvolution package was not available in the environment.
denominator_used: N = 108 tumors (entire Toronto/discovery methylation cohort from GSE207207, matched 1:1 with Methylation_Group labels in MOESM4 Clinical Data). Per-group sample sizes: MPNST_G1=8, MPNST_G2=8, PremalignantNF_G3=29, Benign_NF-G4=20, Sporadic Spinal NF_G5=10, Cutaneous NF_G6=12, Cutaneous NF_G7=21.
assumptions_made: Toronto cohort = full 108-sample GSE207207 discovery cohort described in MOESM4 (no external Toronto-only flag present in the clinical metadata); Immune Score proxied by LUMP-style (Aran et al. 2015 Nat Commun) leukocyte-specific unmethylated CpGs (mean beta across 22 of 39 canonical LUMP CpGs that overlapped the 450K/EPIC probe set shipped in GSE207207_average_beta_values.txt.gz), then Immune Score = 1 - min(mean_beta/0.85, 1) so that higher values = greater immune infiltration; ANOVA is symmetric on linear transforms so LUMP and 1-LUMP yield identical F and p; no covariate adjustment; one-way ANOVA via scipy.stats.f_oneway; no dedicated methylation ESTIMATE or MethylCIBERSORT implementation was available, so result is a LUMP-based approximation rather than an exact reproduction of whatever immune-deconvolution method the authors used for Supp Fig 12e.
step_by_step_analysis:
A. DATA LOADED
   - 41467_2023_38432_MOESM4_ESM.xlsx: 108 rows x 15 columns (Clinical Data). Relevant columns: Tumor_ID, Methylation_Group.
   - GSE207207_average_beta_values.txt.gz: CpG x 108 samples beta matrix (header row lists sample IDs matching Tumor_ID).

B. COHORT DEFINITION
   - Toronto discovery cohort interpreted as the full 108-sample GSE207207 cohort described in MOESM4 (no separate Toronto/validation flag in metadata).
   - All 108 samples had Methylation_Group assignments and beta values -> final analytic N = 108.

C. VARIABLE CODING
   - Methylation_Group: 7 levels (MPNST_G1=8, MPNST_G2=8, PremalignantNF_G3=29, Benign_NF-G4=20, Sporadic Spinal NF_G5=10, Cutaneous NF_G6=12, Cutaneous NF_G7=21).
   - Immune Score: Aran-style LUMP proxy. Used 39-probe canonical LUMP CpG panel (leukocyte-unmethylated). 22 of 39 were present in the supplied beta matrix. For each sample, mean_beta over these 22 probes was computed; LUMP = min(mean_beta / 0.85, 1); Immune Score = 1 - LUMP (higher = more immune infiltrate). ANOVA is invariant to this sign flip.

D. COMPUTATION
   - One-way ANOVA comparing Immune Score across the 7 Methylation_Group levels using scipy.stats.f_oneway(*groups).
   - Degrees of freedom: between groups = 6, within = 101.

E. RESULT
   - F-statistic = 9.3876
   - p-value = 3.5387e-08
   - Group mean (Immune Score): G1=0.450, G2=0.529, G3=0.526, G4=0.505, G5=0.522, G6=0.480, G7=0.500.

F. CAVEATS
   - LUMP is a proxy; paper's Supp Fig 12e may instead use an ESTIMATE-derived or MethylCIBERSORT-derived Immune Score, which could yield a different magnitude. Qualitative difference (significant heterogeneity across groups) is likely robust.
   - Only 22/39 canonical LUMP probes were retained on this array export; using the remaining probes (if available) could nudge the score but not the overall pattern given the large effect size.
   - No covariate adjustment (age, NF1 status, location); ANOVA assumptions (normality/homoscedasticity) were not formally tested but group SDs range 0.017-0.063 and a Kruskal-Wallis nonparametric check could be run as a sensitivity analysis.
